_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Non-traumatic spontaneous (hypertensive) intracerebral hematoma (SICH) requiring surgical intervention such as Craniotomy or Hematoma Evacuation.

preauth.label: Clinical notes including evaluation findings, and planned line of management

claim.label: Glasgow coma score (GCS)/ Pediatric GCS documentation
In case of accident FIR report (optional)

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, indication for procedure, and planned line of management?
Did clinical evaluation and imaging confirm the diagnosis?
Was clinical presentation, GCS/Pediatric GCS score and imaging indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the CT/MRI Brain report indicative of surgery?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?
In case of accidental injury was FIR done?

common_queries: Clinical notes missing or lacking details on history, symptoms, indication, or planned management.
GCS score not documented or not indicative of surgery.
CT/MRI Brain report not indicative of surgery, or imaging not confirming diagnosis.
FIR report not available for cases of accidental injury (if applicable).
Detailed Indoor Case Papers (ICPs) missing or without daily vitals and treatment details.
Detailed Procedure/Operative Notes missing or incomplete.
Discharge summary missing or without follow-up advice.

clinical_key_pointers: Non-traumatic spontaneous (hypertensive) intracerebral hematoma (SICH) is a devastating disease with higher rates of mortality and morbidity than those of ischemic stroke, with an annual incidence of 10–30 per 100,000.
SICH occurs suddenly due to the rupture of vessels affected by hypertension-related degenerative changes in the cerebral lobes, basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem, and cerebellum.
Hypertensive vasculopathy is the most common etiology of spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH).
Some hypertensive hemorrhages occur with exertion or intense emotional activity.

Clinical Manifestations: The neurologic symptoms and signs usually increase gradually over minutes or a few hours. Some patients with ICH are obtunded or comatose when first discovered or upon arrival to the emergency department. Headache, vomiting, and a decreased level of consciousness develop if the hemorrhage becomes sufficiently large. Seizures, Cardiac abnormalities, Neurologic signs vary depending upon the location of the hemorrhage.
additional_information.pre_op_preparation: Basic life support, as well as control of bleeding, seizures, blood pressure (BP), and intracranial pressure, are critical. Medications used include: Anticonvulsants (to prevent seizure recurrence), Antihypertensive agents (to reduce BP and other risk factors of heart disease), Osmotic diuretics (to decrease intracranial pressure in the subarachnoid space).
contraindications: To confirm the diagnosis of SICH, other obvious vascular lesions such as cerebral aneurysm, vascular malformation, arterio-venous fistula, moyamoya disease must be excluded.
